name: Moo Shu Ice Cream & Kitchen
industry: Small-batch ice cream, cafe, catering, and workshops
location: Unit 1, 1130 Wellington Street West, Ottawa, ON K1Y 2Z3
offerings: Small-batch ice cream and vegan ice cream
customerSegments: Ottawa residents seeking distinctive local desserts

observations.title: The official homepage still displays a 2025 market schedule
observations.detail: The current site footer says 2026, but its Markets & Events block still says Parkdale Night Market runs June 4 to October 8, 2025.
observations.implication: A short, current weekly market video and a 2026 schedule refresh would remove ambiguity and turn nearby Hintonburg and Parkdale traffic into visits.

observations.title: The brand has unusually strong process and founder-story material
observations.detail: The official team page says products are dreamed up and made in small batches, with novelty, nostalgia, heritage, and ecology guiding the work. Ottawa Tourism identifies Liz Mok as owner and chef.
observations.implication: Ingredient sourcing, flavour development, and founder-led explainers can become recurring educational and entertaining video series instead of one-off product posts.

observations.title: The conversion journey crosses several external tools
observations.detail: The official navigation sends gift-card buyers to Square and catering visitors to Notion, while workshops and menus remain on the main site.
observations.implication: Platform-specific videos should use one clear destination per campaign so viewers do not have to choose among several paths after watching.

whyNow.title: Peak summer attention plus fresh regional travel coverage
whyNow.detail: CAA North & East Ontario included Moo Shu in a July 7, 2026 Ottawa-area ice-cream guide, while the official shop is operating extended summer hours in Hintonburg. This creates a current window to convert destination interest into trackable visits and catering awareness.

scores.summary: Overall 87/100. The product is highly visual, the founder story is distinctive, and the official contact is public. The main opportunity is to turn rich process and community material into repeatable short-form series while cleaning up stale conversion information.

concepts.title: Why this flavour works
concepts.hook: This ice cream tastes different at three temperatures.
outline: Liz names one current flavour and the memory, heritage ingredient, or local crop behind it.
Macro shots show the small-batch mix, texture, and finished scoop.
A fast taste map labels the first, middle, and finishing notes.
End on the exact in-shop availability window.
concepts.productionNotes: Founder-led, 25 to 35 seconds, natural kitchen sound, macro food shots, caption every ingredient and availability date.

concepts.title: One batch from farm to scoop
concepts.hook: This Ottawa-grown ingredient has 24 hours to become ice cream.
outline: Open at the supplier handoff or ingredient delivery.
Show preparation, base creation, churning, and serving in four rapid chapters.
Name the producer only after approval and link the final flavour to the current menu.
concepts.productionNotes: Shoot vertically across one production day; use a visible clock motif and keep claims limited to verified sourcing.

concepts.title: Can an ice-cream workshop fix this?
concepts.hook: Three friends get one mystery ingredient and one hour.
outline: Introduce a real workshop challenge.
Capture participant reactions, a quick teaching moment, and the final reveal.
Close with the workshop and catering booking path.
concepts.productionNotes: Use real participants with releases; create one hero reel plus three short reaction cutdowns.

outreach.subject: A short-form series built around Moo Shu's flavour process
outreach.opening: Hi Liz, I was looking through Moo Shu's current site and your team page's description of novelty, nostalgia, heritage, and ecology is an unusually strong video brief in itself.
outreach.body: I also noticed the homepage still shows the Parkdale Night Market's 2025 dates, even as the Hintonburg shop is in peak summer season and CAA featured Moo Shu in its July 7 local ice-cream guide. Que Media could turn one production day into a recurring founder-led series that follows a flavour from ingredient to scoop, then pair each video with one current visit, workshop, or catering action. The goal would be useful, specific storytelling, not generic food glamour shots.
outreach.callToAction: Would you be open to a 15-minute conversation if I send a three-video outline based on one flavour you are serving now?

sources.label: Ottawa ice-cream guide and owner identification

name: Beyond the Pale Brewing Company
industry: Independent craft brewery, taprooms, restaurant, retail, and events
location: 250 City Centre Avenue, Unit 108, Ottawa, ON; second taproom at 21 George Street, Ottawa, ON K1N 8W5
offerings: City Centre taproom with 16 taps, full kitchen, and patio
ByWard Market taproom and event space
Retail cans, home kegs, and merchandise
Private events and recurring public events

observations.title: Two venues create a large but split content calendar
observations.detail: The official site maintains separate event paths for City Centre and ByWard, while the about page positions both the brewery and the newer market location.
observations.implication: A consistent two-location video format could help customers immediately understand where each event, menu item, or experience is happening.

observations.title: The current event pipeline is ready-made for episodic video
observations.detail: The official July 2026 calendars list live music and brunch at ByWard and an Outside the Box trivia event at City Centre on July 22.
observations.implication: Weekly preview, host-introduction, and next-day recap videos can compound audience recognition instead of treating each event as an isolated poster.

observations.title: A visible hours error weakens otherwise strong local conversion information
observations.detail: The official about page lists City Centre's Sunday hours as 11 AM to 10 AM, while the same page describes a full-service taproom.
observations.implication: A basic information cleanup should accompany any campaign so high-performing videos land on accurate visit details.

whyNow.title: Active July programming at both Ottawa venues
whyNow.detail: The official calendars show ByWard brunch and patio live music on July 19 and City Centre trivia on July 22, creating an immediate production window for event-led video and a repeatable weekly series.

scores.summary: Overall 82/100. Brewing, food, staff, live events, and two distinct venues offer deep visual inventory. The primary opportunity is a recognizable multi-location series. Response is moderated because the established brewery likely has existing marketing resources.

concepts.title: BTP This Week: two venues in 30 seconds
concepts.hook: City Centre or ByWard? Here is the move this week.
outline: Split-screen the week's strongest event at each venue.
Show one food or beer pairing for each location.
End with venue-specific date, time, and address.
concepts.productionNotes: One repeatable template, shot weekly; strong location labels and no generic stock beer footage.

concepts.title: One tap, one story
concepts.hook: This beer started with one ingredient we almost did not use.
outline: A brewer introduces one current pour.
Show the ingredient, process moment, and final pour.
Pair it with one current menu item and say where it is available.
concepts.productionNotes: Founder or brewer on camera, 30 seconds, close sound design, macro ingredient shots.

concepts.title: Before the doors open
concepts.hook: What it takes to turn a heritage courtyard into tonight's event space.
outline: Time-lapse the ByWard setup.
Introduce the event host or performer.
Show the first guest arrival and one atmosphere payoff.
concepts.productionNotes: Shoot one real event from setup through opening; make one 40-second hero cut and three six-second reminders.

outreach.subject: A two-location weekly video format for BTP
outreach.opening: Hi Shane, BTP's July calendar made one content opportunity unusually clear: City Centre and ByWard each have their own reason to visit in the same week.
outreach.body: The current pages list ByWard brunch and patio music alongside City Centre's July 22 trivia, but a first-time customer still has to work through separate calendars to decide where to go. Que Media could build a repeatable 'BTP This Week' short-form series that gives each venue one strong visual moment, one pairing, and one exact action. I also noticed the About page lists Sunday's City Centre close as 10 AM, so I would pair the campaign with a quick conversion-detail audit rather than send traffic to uncertain information.
outreach.callToAction: Can I send a sample 30-second storyboard using the next event at each location?

sources.label: Official about, locations, contact, and social links

name: Dominion City Brewing Co.
industry: Independent craft brewery, taproom, direct-to-consumer retail, and events
offerings: Curated Canadian beer, wine, cider, and food through Friends of the Dominion
Mobile shopping app, subscriptions, and events
customerSegments: Customers seeking Canadian independent beverage producers

observations.title: The brand has several real stories beyond the beer can
observations.detail: The official About page documents Ontario ingredients, Kitigan Zibi collaboration, community partnerships, and a values-led team. Friends of the Dominion adds independent Canadian producers.
observations.implication: A recurring people-and-place series can make sourcing, collaboration, and community impact visible without relying on generic tasting notes.

observations.title: Stubby's adds a new food-led audience and production setting
observations.detail: The official news page introduced Stubby's as the brewery's in-house pizza offering on April 15, 2026 and followed with a June 30 pizza party promoted on June 19.
observations.implication: Beer-plus-pizza builds, staff challenges, and event recaps can reach customers who do not begin with craft beer as their main interest.

observations.title: Product, taproom, delivery, app, and events compete for attention
observations.detail: The official site separately promotes beer, taproom visits, local delivery, an app, Friends of the Dominion, subscriptions, and news events.
observations.implication: Each short-form video should resolve to one measurable action, with a campaign map that prevents several calls to action from appearing at once.

whyNow.title: A new pizza program is being activated through live events
whyNow.detail: Dominion City introduced Stubby's Pizza in April and promoted a June 30, 2026 pre-Canada Day Grown-Ass Pizza Party in a June 19 news post. This is a recent product-launch and event signal with obvious repeat-content potential.

scores.summary: Overall 82/100. Brewing, pizza, collaborations, community work, and a distinct East End setting give Que Media deep material. Opportunity is strategic rather than remedial; response is moderated because this is an established brand with mature channels.

concepts.title: The beer ingredient with an address
concepts.hook: This beer starts on an Ontario farm, not in our brewery.
outline: Identify one verified local ingredient or producer.
Move from raw ingredient to brew-floor process.
Finish with the current beer and where it is pouring.
concepts.productionNotes: Film a vertical mini-documentary with source approval; keep location and producer captions precise.

concepts.title: Stubby's build of the week
concepts.hook: The pizza made for this exact Dominion City pour.
outline: Chef builds one pizza in fast closeups.
Brewer explains the pairing in one sentence.
Staff taste and choose the best bite-plus-sip order.
concepts.productionNotes: 25 seconds, kitchen sound first, minimal voiceover, explicit taproom availability.

concepts.title: Friends of the Dominion: pass the bottle
concepts.hook: Three independent Canadian makers, one shelf, one reason each.
outline: A staff member selects three current products.
Each gets a five-second maker story and use occasion.
Close on the curated shelf and pickup eligibility.
concepts.productionNotes: Tag participating producers after approval and repurpose collaborative footage across accounts.

outreach.subject: Turning Stubby's and Dominion City's producer stories into a repeatable series
outreach.opening: Hi Josh, the recent Stubby's launch and June 30 pizza party make Dominion City's next content chapter feel bigger than another beer-release post.
outreach.body: Your official story already gives the raw material: Ontario ingredients, independent Canadian producers through Friends of the Dominion, community partnerships, and now an in-house pizza program. Que Media could turn those into two repeatable short-form formats, one following an ingredient or partner and one pairing a Stubby's build with a current pour. Each episode would point to one action only, such as taproom visit, current release, or event, so the app, delivery, shop, and event options do not compete in the same video.
outreach.callToAction: Would it be useful if I sent a three-episode pilot outline built around one current beer, one Stubby's pizza, and one Friends producer?

sources.label: Official company story, values, contact, and social links

name: House of TARG
industry: Independent live music venue, classic arcade, pinball room, perogi restaurant, and private events
offerings: Live rock, punk, metal, indie, and synth shows
More than 40 classic arcade and pinball games
Weekend Family Free-Play and Sunday After Dark free-play
customerSegments: Ottawa live-music fans and local bands
Classic arcade and competitive pinball players
Students, friend groups, and date-night customers

observations.title: The venue serves several audiences on one fast-moving calendar
observations.detail: The official schedule moves from a weekend punk festival to all-ages Tuesday Arcade, a monthly pinball tournament, and another live show within six days. Family Free-Play also runs every Saturday and Sunday.
observations.implication: A recurring daily video briefing can route families, gamers, and concert audiences to the right experience without flattening the venue into one generic arcade-bar message.

observations.title: Pinball, perogies, and live music create three ownable content pillars
observations.detail: The official site describes more than 40 games, a perogi menu, and a live-music program. CityNews reports that the founders intentionally combined those elements into a venue Granger described as part Disneyland and part CBGB.
observations.implication: Que Media can build recognizable recurring formats around staff expertise, food craft, and performance energy instead of relying on event-poster slideshows.

observations.title: High-intent actions are distributed across several destinations
observations.detail: The official experience separates event listings, third-party ticket links, free-play details, private-party information, and the contact page. Regular groups are first come, first served while private-party requests use a separate path.
observations.implication: Each short-form post should carry one audience-specific call to action and one direct destination so viewers know whether to buy a ticket, plan a family visit, or inquire about a private event.

whyNow.title: Two recurring arcade products and a live show land on consecutive nights
whyNow.detail: The official calendar lists all-ages Tuesday Arcade on July 21, the monthly pinball tournament and $5 video-game free-play on July 22, and a three-band bill on July 23. The next three days provide a credible, immediate pilot window across the venue's core audiences.

scores.summary: Overall 90/100. House of TARG is unusually well matched to Que Media: kinetic games, food craft, staff personality, live performance, recurring events, and strong local identity all support entertaining and educational short-form video. A public co-owner email and immediate event cadence improve outreach readiness.
dimensions: Clear ticket, visit, and event conversion paths: 19/20

concepts.title: One quarter, one riff, one perogi
concepts.hook: Three reasons Ottawa keeps coming back to one basement.
outline: Match-cut a coin entering a game to a musician plugging in.
Move through one perogi finishing shot and one crowd reaction.
End on that night's exact offer and ticket or visit destination.
concepts.productionNotes: Capture vertical macro details, practical neon, clean kitchen audio, and one licensed performance moment. Build versions for concerts, family free-play, and private events.

concepts.title: Wizard's game of the week
concepts.hook: You have 30 seconds to learn the machine everyone walks past.
outline: A staff host names one unusual machine and its objective.
Demonstrate one satisfying mechanic or beginner tactic.
Challenge viewers to beat a visible score during the next free-play session.
concepts.productionNotes: Use an overhead control shot, one gameplay close-up, captions for loud-room viewing, and a repeatable scoreboard graphic.

concepts.title: Tonight at TARG
concepts.hook: If you have one free night in Ottawa, here is what is happening downstairs.
outline: Open on one band riff, game sound, or perogi sizzle tied to the night.
State the audience, doors, cover, and one reason the event is different.
Close with one direct ticket or walk-in call to action.
concepts.productionNotes: Create a 15-second template that staff can refresh daily from a monthly capture bank while preserving handcrafted poster art and the venue's DIY voice.

outreach.subject: A three-night TARG content pilot for arcade, pinball, and live music
outreach.opening: Hi Yogi, TARG's next three nights are a perfect snapshot of what makes the venue hard to copy: Tuesday Arcade on July 21, the monthly pinball tournament on July 22, and a three-band bill on July 23.
outreach.body: Que Media could turn that run into one compact pilot built around TARG's three visual worlds: games, perogies, and live music. I would start with a 15-second Tonight at TARG briefing, a staff-led Wizard's Game of the Week, and a match-cut piece that moves from coin drop to guitar plug-in to perogi finish. Each version would send one audience to one action, rather than asking families, pinball players, and showgoers to decode the whole calendar at once.
outreach.callToAction: Would it be useful if I sent a one-page shot list mapped to the July 21 to 23 schedule?

sources.label: Official homepage, live schedule, recurring free-play, address, email, and phone
Official contact, group policy, and family information

name: Stolen Goods Cocktail Bar
industry: Independent cocktail bar and late-night small-plate restaurant
offerings: Rotating themed menus and local pop-ups
customerSegments: Followers of local chef and bartender collaborations

observations.title: The most important current story is not on the homepage
observations.detail: Ottawa Business Journal reported on March 4, 2026 that the owners will close the Sparks Street bar at the end of 2026 and are planning a next venture. The current official homepage lists scheduled closures but does not present the final-year or next-concept story.
observations.implication: A deliberate final-year documentary arc can turn a potentially confusing closure narrative into community participation, reservations, and an audience that follows the owners to their next concept.

observations.title: The official homepage relies on archival 2022 photography
observations.detail: The official page's image filenames prominently reference May, August, and October 2022 while the business is now in its final announced year at this address.
observations.implication: Fresh motion assets can document the current staff, pop-ups, guests, and final-year menu instead of letting the 2026 story depend on launch-era stills.

observations.title: The team's personalities and local sourcing are strong differentiators
observations.detail: Professional profiles identify Mike Campbell as co-owner and general manager, Adam Ghor as co-owner and chef, and describe the bar's thoughtful details, raccoon motif, Canadian sourcing, and rotating collaboration model.
observations.implication: Character-led, slightly irreverent video can make the people and decision-making behind each menu more memorable than polished cocktail beauty shots alone.

whyNow.title: A recent pop-up cycle is feeding a final-year narrative
whyNow.detail: A public mirror of the bar's posts records a June 22, 2026 Summer Clean Out promotion and a June 17 pop-up update. The official site confirms the June 27 to July 10 closure window, while Ottawa Business Journal confirms the bar's end-of-2026 close and owners' next-venture plans.

scores.summary: Overall 93/100. Stolen Goods combines an intensely visual product, identifiable operators, irreverent personality, pop-ups, a public email, and a time-bound final-year story. It is the strongest narrative opportunity in this group.

concepts.title: The last menu at 106 Sparks
concepts.hook: This drink will disappear when this room does.
outline: Mike or Adam introduces one item and the decision behind it.
Show the build with one unexpected technique or ingredient.
End with the finite availability and reservation path.
concepts.productionNotes: Create a numbered final-year series; intimate macro visuals, real bar sound, restrained subtitles.

concepts.title: Raccoon review board
concepts.hook: Our least qualified regular approves the next pop-up.
outline: Use the established raccoon motif as a recurring silent character.
The team pitches a pop-up dish or drink to the mascot.
A fast verdict reveals the date and collaborator.
concepts.productionNotes: Dry humour, practical prop, six to fifteen seconds, built for recurring collaborator reveals.

concepts.title: What comes with us
concepts.hook: Five things from Stolen Goods that survive the move.
outline: Each owner chooses one value, ritual, object, or technique.
Show it working in the current space.
Do not reveal confidential next-venue details; end with a follow prompt.
concepts.productionNotes: Founder-led documentary, 45 seconds, shoot in chapters over the remaining year.

outreach.subject: A final-year short-form story for Stolen Goods
outreach.opening: Hi Mike, the end-of-2026 announcement gives Stolen Goods something almost no bar can manufacture: a real, finite story with a next chapter already taking shape.
outreach.body: The current site still leads with 2022-era photography and scheduled closures, while the final-year plan, pop-ups, and next venture live mostly in press and social updates. Que Media could document the year as a numbered short-form series: the last menus at 106 Sparks, the collaborators who shaped the room, and the values or rituals that will follow you next. The raccoon motif also gives the quick promotional cuts a character that already belongs to the bar.
outreach.callToAction: Would you be open to seeing a six-episode outline that starts with the next pop-up and does not disclose anything you are not ready to share?

sources.label: Official location, email, hours, social links, and closure dates
Public mirror of recent social posts

name: Escape Manor Ottawa
industry: Immersive entertainment, hospitality, live events, and corporate experiences
neighborhood: Elgin Street, Hintonburg, Nepean, and Carp
location: Primary hospitality venue: 292 Elgin Street, Ottawa, ON K2P 1M3; additional Ottawa-area experiences at 982 Wellington Street West, 900 Greenbank Road, and 3929 Carp Road
offerings: Escape rooms at four Ottawa-area locations
Manor Lounge food, cocktails, brunch, lunch, and dinner
Live music, trivia, magic, and themed events
Corporate events, team building, parties, and retreats
customerSegments: Food, drink, live-music, and nightlife audiences

observations.title: The offer is broader than the name suggests
observations.detail: The official Ottawa page leads with brunch, lunch, dinner, drinks, escapes, darts, and live music, and lists four Ottawa-area experience locations.
observations.implication: Short-form content should segment by occasion, such as date night, family hour, or corporate social, rather than attempt to explain every product in one brand video.

observations.title: The homepage has many promotions but no official TikTok or YouTube link
observations.detail: The current highlight rail includes $20 Tuesdays, Family Hour, Summer Social Thursdays, trivia, wine nights, live music, magic, and other offers. The official footer links X, Facebook, and Instagram only.
observations.implication: A video-first campaign can turn the dense promotion set into recurring characters and occasion-based stories, while testing whether vertical-video distribution should expand beyond the currently linked channels.

observations.title: The environments already contain narrative hooks
observations.detail: The official page describes scenarios including Sherlock, Prohibited, Flight 69, Carnival, The Apothecary, and Diefenbunker missions, each with a specific story premise.
observations.implication: Spoiler-safe trailers, staff-in-character sketches, and first-three-seconds challenges can promote rooms without revealing puzzles.

whyNow.title: Active summer offers and sold-out dates create immediate demand signals
whyNow.detail: The official booking experience was serving July 2026 inventory and marked July 15 and 16 sold out. The Ottawa homepage concurrently promotes Summer Social Thursdays, Family Hour, live music, and other seasonal offers.

scores.summary: Overall 85/100. The product is almost purpose-built for entertaining video and has many trackable booking actions. The response score is lower because Escape Manor already has a named CMO and operates across several cities, so Que Media needs a sharp Ottawa execution proposal rather than a general marketing pitch.

concepts.title: Choose your Ottawa night in five seconds
concepts.hook: You have five seconds: cocktails, darts, or a missing Sherlock?
outline: Present three visual doors tied to real Elgin offers.
A viewer-choice transition opens one experience.
End with one date-specific booking action.
concepts.productionNotes: Interactive edit with three variants; shoot vertical at Elgin and use exact product labels from the official booking system.

concepts.title: Game master red flags
concepts.hook: Three things your team does before it loses an escape room.
outline: A real game master demonstrates a common non-spoiler mistake.
Cut to a funny team reenactment.
Give one useful communication tip and identify an appropriate room difficulty.
concepts.productionNotes: Staff-led educational comedy, 20 to 30 seconds, avoid puzzle details and guest-identifiable footage without release.

concepts.title: The room trailer without a single spoiler
concepts.hook: Sherlock vanished. You have 60 minutes and this is all we can show you.
outline: Use lighting, props, reactions, and one line of premise.
Show the clock and team energy without puzzle solutions.
Close with location, group fit, and booking link.
concepts.productionNotes: Cinematic 25-second trailer, practical sound, controlled shallow-focus shots, version for each priority room.

outreach.subject: An Ottawa video system for Escape Manor's many summer offers
outreach.opening: Hi Steve, Escape Manor's Ottawa homepage currently asks one brand to carry escapes, Manor Lounge, darts, bocce, Family Hour, Summer Social Thursdays, live music, and corporate groups.
outreach.body: That breadth is a strength, but it also makes the content job more like audience routing than simple promotion. Que Media could build three repeatable Ottawa-only formats: spoiler-safe room trailers, game-master comedy that teaches teamwork, and 'choose your night' videos that route viewers to one live booking action. The current July inventory and sold-out dates provide a useful test window, and the work would support your existing marketing direction rather than replace it.
outreach.callToAction: Would you be open to a compact pilot proposal for one Elgin offer and one Hintonburg room, with booking clicks as the success measure?

sources.label: Official Ottawa experiences, locations, highlights, and socials
Current CMO and Rogers Centre partnership

name: The Merry Dairy
industry: Ice cream, frozen custard, food trucks, catering, wholesale, and community events
offerings: Original-recipe ice cream and frozen custard
Three food trucks and event visits
Catering, school programs, fundraisers, and wholesale
Ice-cream cakes, pints, sandwiches, merchandise, and cookbook
customerSegments: Customers managing nut and sesame allergies

observations.title: The business has a deep owned-story archive
observations.detail: The official history covers the 2012 food-truck launch, the Fairmont shop, a three-truck fleet, allergy-safe production, over 100 flavours, community work, and the Great Scoops cookbook.
observations.implication: Que Media can adapt proven owned stories into short video instead of inventing campaign themes from scratch.

observations.title: The website is rich but cognitively dense
observations.detail: The current navigation exposes dozens of paths across shop, pints, delivery, gift options, Name Days, truck tracking, exchanges, music, catering, schools, fundraisers, merchandise, and blog content.
observations.implication: Video campaigns should reduce each story to one audience and one next step, while profile links route visitors to a purpose-built landing choice.

observations.title: The current Name Days mechanic is built for daily participation
observations.detail: The official Name Days page says the 2026 summer edition returned July 17 and directs people to the homepage and social channels to see the selected names and claim a free cone.
observations.implication: A consistent daily reveal format can increase recognition and sharing during the exact period when the promotion is active.

whyNow.title: Summer Name Days returned July 17
whyNow.detail: The official page says Names of the Day returned Friday, July 17, 2026, with at least four names selected per day and social channels used for the reveal. The campaign is live now and naturally supports daily short-form content.

scores.summary: Overall 85/100. The product, trucks, founder, staff, community rituals, allergy education, and active daily promotion are excellent content inputs. Opportunity is lower because The Merry Dairy already maintains a mature story ecosystem; the value is efficient video adaptation and campaign clarity.

concepts.title: Name of the Day reveal machine
concepts.hook: If your name appears in three seconds, your cone is free today.
outline: Use a physical scoop, truck, or pint-label reveal device.
Show the selected names in large accessible type.
State the claim window and Fairmont address.
concepts.productionNotes: Batch-shoot the reusable mechanism, edit names daily, add captions and alt-text-ready stills.

concepts.title: Allergy-safe does not mean boring
concepts.hook: Three things that never enter this ice-cream facility, and 100 flavours that do.
outline: Marlene explains the peanut-, tree-nut-, and sesame-free commitment.
Show separate dairy and plant-based creations without making unverified medical claims.
Close with a clear invitation to review current flavour information.
concepts.productionNotes: Educational, calm tone, exact wording approved by the business, no guarantee language beyond the official claim.

concepts.title: Where is the Merry Dairy truck?
concepts.hook: You have ten seconds to find today's truck before it finds your neighbourhood.
outline: Open on a stylized Ottawa map.
Cut from the shop to the truck route and destination.
Feature one real event host or neighbourhood and the tracking link.
concepts.productionNotes: Repeatable map animation plus same-day phone footage; make location and service times explicit.

outreach.subject: A daily video reveal for The Merry Dairy's live Name Days campaign
outreach.opening: Hi Marlene, your Name Days page says the summer campaign returned July 17 with at least four names selected each day, which is almost a ready-made short-form series.
outreach.body: The Merry Dairy already has more real stories than most brands: three trucks, allergy-safe production, over 100 flavours, community partnerships, the cookbook, and the Fairmont shop. Que Media could turn the live Name Days mechanic into a recognizable daily reveal, then use the same production day for an allergy-education piece and a truck-tracking format. Each video would point to one action, which is useful because the current site navigation serves many different audiences at once.
outreach.callToAction: Would you like a sample seven-day Name Days template that your team could approve once and update quickly each morning?

sources.label: Official contact, social links, and offer navigation

name: Rockin' Johnny's Diner
ownership: Enzo Mastromattei, publicly identified franchise owner; Anthony Joseph, Westgate buyer and operator since 2005. The current Merivale legal entity is unverified.
industry: Retro family diner, all-day breakfast, burgers, comfort food, and milkshakes
emailLabel: Official Kanata email; confirm Merivale routing

decisionMaker.name: Enzo Mastromattei
decisionMaker.role: Franchise owner and founder of the original Westgate location

offerings: Burgers, sandwiches, and classic diner plates
customerSegments: Customers drawn to retro Ottawa nostalgia

observations.title: The Merivale reopening combines a fresh launch with more than three decades of local recognition
observations.detail: Ottawa Business Journal reported that the former Westgate operation was moving to 1651 Merivale Road with a more modern, slightly more upscale interpretation of the familiar Rockin' Johnny's atmosphere. The retained wall car and returning former employees give the launch visible continuity rather than a generic new-restaurant story.
observations.implication: A launch series can show what moved, what changed, and which familiar people and details returned, giving both former regulars and new nearby customers a reason to visit.

observations.title: Public ownership and operator continuity are unusually well documented
observations.detail: Current local reporting identifies Enzo Mastromattei as franchise owner and says he opened the original Westgate location in 1991. Anthony Joseph bought that location in 2005, remained its familiar day-to-day owner-operator, and joined Mastromattei in choosing the Merivale site.
observations.implication: Founder and operator-led storytelling can credibly connect the original 1991 diner, the Westgate community, and the new Merivale chapter without relying on invented brand history.

observations.title: The official website has not caught up with the new location
observations.detail: The official site still says the Westgate location is closed and that the business hopes to reopen in the area; it does not list 1651 Merivale Road or a Merivale menu. Its HTTPS certificate also does not match the public domain, so standard secure clients reject the HTTPS version while the HTTP page remains available.
observations.implication: Before launch content drives traffic, the site should add the new address, hours, menu, and contact path and restore valid HTTPS so searchers can confidently find and act on current information.

observations.title: The relocation story is rooted in loyal customers and long-serving staff
observations.detail: Kitchissippi Times documented weekly customers of 14 years and employees with more than a decade at Westgate. Ottawa Business Journal later reported that some employees displaced by the mall closure would return at Merivale.
observations.implication: Consent-based portraits of returning staff and regulars can make the relocation emotionally specific while avoiding a launch campaign built only on food glamour shots.

whyNow.title: The new Merivale diner opened in mid-July 2026
whyNow.detail: Ottawa Business Journal announced the 1651 Merivale Road reopening on July 10 for the following week. Local customer reports indicate the doors opened July 15 and document a visit on July 18, creating an immediate launch window while nearby awareness and former-customer nostalgia are still active.

scores.summary: Overall 90/100. The just-opened Merivale location has a time-sensitive launch story, recognizable retro visuals, named public leadership roles, returning staff, and more than three decades of Ottawa history. The biggest immediate gap is that the official website still presents Westgate as closed and does not give customers current Merivale information.

concepts.title: The car moved too
concepts.hook: Westgate closed, but this piece of Rockin' Johnny's made the move.
outline: Open on a tight detail of the familiar car mounted inside the new diner.
Let Enzo or Anthony explain one object, one staff return, and one deliberate change in the Merivale design.
Reveal the full 1651 Merivale Road space and finish with current opening information.
concepts.productionNotes: Thirty to forty seconds, owner-led, use archival Westgate images only with permission, and make the address readable on screen.

concepts.title: Westgate regulars, Merivale firsts
concepts.hook: Their old table disappeared, but their weekly order did not.
outline: Feature one consenting former Westgate regular or returning employee.
Recreate the familiar order in the new dining room.
End with the detail that feels the same and the one feature that feels new.
concepts.productionNotes: Documentary tone, written releases for every featured person, no filming uninvolved diners, and one story per episode.

concepts.title: New diner, same order
concepts.hook: Can the Merivale kitchen pass a 1991 breakfast test?
outline: Choose one recognizable breakfast, burger, or milkshake from the brand's history.
Show the build in three fast kitchen chapters.
Have Enzo, Anthony, or a returning staff member explain what stayed consistent and invite viewers to try it at Merivale.
concepts.productionNotes: Vertical food close-ups, natural kitchen sound, captions throughout, and verify the item is on the current Merivale menu before filming.

outreach.subject: A Merivale launch story built around what came back from Westgate
outreach.opening: Hi Enzo, I saw your Ottawa Business Journal interview about reopening Rockin' Johnny's at 1651 Merivale Road and bringing back former Westgate staff while giving the diner a more modern look.
outreach.body: There is a strong short-form launch story in the contrast: the car on the wall and long-time people came back, but the room has changed for a new neighbourhood. Que Media could turn one owner-led production day into three pieces: what moved from Westgate, a returning staff or regular story, and one signature-order build. I also noticed the official site still says Westgate is closed and does not list Merivale, so I would pair the videos with a focused launch-information cleanup rather than send viewers to an outdated page.
outreach.callToAction: Would you be open to a 15-minute conversation if I send a three-video Merivale launch outline first?

name: Sukkar House
industry: Dessert cafe, crepes, waffles, ice cream, specialty drinks, coffee, and breakfast
offerings: Sweet crepes, including sushi crepe and Sukkar House Fettuccine
Waffles, bubble waffles, and mini pancakes
Ice cream and Arabic ice cream
Fresh juices, smoothies, mojitos, and milkshakes
Hot and cold coffee, tea, and Turkish coffee
customerSegments: Meadowlands, Merivale, and Nepean dessert customers
Customers seeking pistachio, qishta, Arabic ice cream, and Middle Eastern-influenced flavours
Breakfast, coffee, juice, and smoothie customers

observations.title: The launch burst did not become an always-on content system
observations.detail: The complete public Instagram grid contained 23 posts from January 3 to May 15, 2026: 10 Reels, 12 single-image posts, and one carousel. Sixteen posts were published in January, but no new grid post appeared in the 69 days between May 15 and the July 23 research capture.
observations.implication: A batch-produced weekly system would preserve the strong launch energy without requiring daily filming and would give current delivery offers a consistent owned-media counterpart.

observations.title: Visible interaction exists, but the account is not converting it through an owned destination
observations.detail: The 23-post sample showed 497 visible likes and 170 visible comments, averaging 21.6 likes and 7.4 comments per post. Against the 388 followers visible on July 23, that is a directional 7.5 visible interactions per post per 100 current followers, not a reach-based engagement rate. The profile exposes a phone number but no website, menu, or ordering link.
observations.implication: The next content run should keep the response-friendly product hooks while adding one maintained link destination for directions, current hours, menu, and ordering.

observations.title: The shared-location identity needs one repeated answer
observations.detail: An official March 26 post says Sukkar House is inside Prince Bakery & Sweets at 885 Meadowlands Drive. OttawaEats separately lists Sukkar House at that address, while the profile bio names only the street address.
observations.implication: Every location-led video should show the exterior, say "inside Prince Bakery & Sweets," and finish with the same map wording so a first-time visitor knows exactly what to look for.

observations.title: The menu is highly filmable, but the public conversion journey is fragmented
observations.detail: The current DoorDash menu spans breakfast, coffee, crepes, waffles, juices, smoothies, milkshakes, and ice cream, including signature items such as Happiness Boom, Sukkar House Fettuccine, and the Four Seasons Waffle. A current Uber Eats listing coexists with a separate older listing marked closed as of February 20, 2026, and neither delivery destination is linked from Instagram.
observations.implication: Content should organize the broad menu into recognizable occasions and signature series, then send each video to one tested destination instead of leaving customers to resolve multiple marketplace listings.

whyNow.title: A newly incorporated dessert brand has gone quiet during its first summer
whyNow.detail: A federal-registry-derived record lists Sukkar House Inc. as incorporated on December 14, 2025, the official account teased "Coming soon" on January 3, 2026, and the latest public grid post was May 15. As of July 23, the delivery menu remained active while the grid had been quiet for 69 days, creating a timely relaunch window before the first-summer awareness opportunity passes.

scores.summary: Overall 89/100. Sukkar House has exceptionally visual preparation, signature products, late-day occasions, and a warm visible response pattern. The strongest opportunity is to restart a dormant launch account with a repeatable content system that clarifies the shared location and connects every video to one maintained visit or order destination.

dimensions.name: Visual product transformations
dimensions.evidence: Crepes, waffles, layered drinks, fruit, ice cream, sauces, and toppings create clear before-and-after and ASMR sequences.

dimensions.name: Signature product and menu variety
dimensions.evidence: Named products such as Happiness Boom, Sukkar House Fettuccine, and Four Seasons Waffle can anchor recognizable series.

dimensions.name: Repeatable short-form formats
dimensions.evidence: Preparation, flavour choice, customer reaction, late-night occasion, and location-guide formats can recur without repeating the same story.

dimensions.name: Local and cultural distinctiveness
dimensions.evidence: Arabic ice cream, qishta, pistachio, Turkish coffee, and the inside-Prince location provide more specific territory than generic dessert glamour.

dimensions.name: Trackable visit and order actions
dimensions.evidence: Phone, in-store visits, pickup, and delivery can support concrete calls to action once the destination is standardized.

dimensions.name: Recent-launch narrative
dimensions.evidence: The December 2025 incorporation and January 2026 teaser create a credible new-brand story.

dimensions.name: Cadence recovery need
dimensions.evidence: Sixteen January posts were followed by a 69-day grid gap after May 15.

dimensions.name: Identity and location clarity
dimensions.evidence: The concept operates inside Prince Bakery & Sweets, but that relationship is not stated in the profile bio.

dimensions.name: Owned conversion surface
dimensions.evidence: The profile has no external website, menu, or ordering link despite active third-party delivery listings.

dimensions.name: Measurable campaign path
dimensions.evidence: Delivery and visit actions are measurable, but owner-authorized analytics and a canonical landing destination are not yet available.

dimensions.name: Public Instagram route
dimensions.evidence: A verified professional Instagram account is the natural first channel.

dimensions.name: Public phone route
dimensions.evidence: The business phone is published directly in the official Instagram bio.

dimensions.name: Local independent fit
dimensions.evidence: The single Ottawa location and early-stage brand footprint make a compact pilot proportionate.

dimensions.name: Specific timely opening
dimensions.evidence: The first-summer cadence gap and shared-location issue support a precise, non-generic message.

dimensions.name: Decision-maker reachability
dimensions.evidence: No public business email or professionally verified named decision maker was found.

concepts.title: Sukkar is inside Prince
concepts.hook: Sukkar House is inside Prince Bakery—here is exactly how to find it.
outline: Open on the 885 Meadowlands exterior and show the Prince Bakery & Sweets sign.
Use a five-second walk-in path to the Sukkar counter.
Finish on one signature dessert, the phone number, and one current order or visit destination.
concepts.productionNotes: Vertical 20-second location guide; keep exterior signage, entrance, counter, and map wording consistent across every cutdown.

concepts.title: Build the Happiness Boom
concepts.hook: This is what a Happiness Boom looks like before the first drizzle.
outline: Start with the empty plate and name each verified component as it is added.
Use close preparation sound for crepe, fruit, qishta, ice cream, and finishing sauce.
Reveal the full portion beside a person for scale and end with the exact current availability.
concepts.productionNotes: 30-second hero Reel plus six modular ingredient cutdowns; verify the recipe wording with the business before publishing.

concepts.title: Pick your late-night dessert
concepts.hook: You get one choice: pistachio, Lotus, Oreo, or Belgian chocolate.
outline: Build four fast quadrants using the same waffle or crepe base.
Give each flavour a distinct texture shot and on-screen label.
Invite viewers to comment their pick, then direct the final frame to one maintained in-store or delivery route.
concepts.productionNotes: Batch film one base preparation and four finishes; export a master comparison, four flavour cuts, and Story polls.

outreach.opening: Hi Sukkar House team, I reviewed your full public grid and current menu because the launch has the ingredients for a strong Ottawa dessert series.
outreach.body: Sixteen of your 23 posts landed in January, and the grid has been quiet since May 15 even though the current delivery menu still features signature products like Happiness Boom, Sukkar House Fettuccine, and the Four Seasons Waffle. I also noticed the location detail that can make or break a first visit: one official post says Sukkar is inside Prince Bakery & Sweets, but the bio does not explain that or link to a menu. Que Media could turn one shoot into three repeatable systems—a clear inside-Prince location guide, a signature-build series, and four-way flavour choices—each ending on one current visit or order action.
outreach.callToAction: Would you be open to a 15-minute conversation if I send the three-video shot plan first?
